subtopic_detail: Pre-operative correction in hypertrophic pyloric stenosis
stem: A 5-week-old first-born male presents with 2 weeks of progressively forceful non-bilious vomiting after every feed and visible peristalsis across the upper abdomen. He has lost 6% of his birth weight. An ultrasound by the on-call radiologist confirms hypertrophic pyloric stenosis with a pyloric muscle thickness of 4.2 mm and channel length of 18 mm. Paediatric surgery has accepted him for pyloromyotomy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158, RR 42, T 36.8, SpO2 99%, capillary refill 3 seconds, anterior fontanelle mildly depressed
data_table.Investigations: Venous blood gas pH 7.52, pCO2 50, HCO3 38, Na 132, K 3.0, Cl 92, glucose 4.1, creatinine 38

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step before theatre?

options.text: Proceed directly to theatre today as the procedure is brief and any electrolyte derangement will correct intraoperatively
options.rationale: Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis predisposes to post-operative apnoea and arrhythmia; correction precedes anaesthesia. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Resuscitate with 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus then maintenance with 4% dextrose and 0.18% sodium chloride until theatre
options.rationale: Hypotonic fluids should not be used; 0.18% saline-dextrose worsens hyponatraemia and is no longer Australian standard maintenance. (Source: RCH CPG: Intravenous Fluids)

options.text: Resuscitate with sodium chloride 0.9% then maintenance sodium chloride 0.45% with 5% dextrose and potassium chloride 20 mmol/L before theatre
options.rationale: Targets bicarbonate under 30 mmol/L and chloride above 100 mmol/L; potassium replacement guards against post-operative apnoea and arrhythmia, and resuscitation precedes pyloromyotomy. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Replace the fluid deficit with oral electrolyte solution by feeding cup over 24 hours then proceed to surgery once tolerating feeds
options.rationale: Persistent gastric outlet obstruction precludes oral correction; ongoing vomiting will worsen alkalosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg slowly to partially correct the metabolic alkalosis before theatre transfer
options.rationale: The metabolic alkalosis is driven by chloride and potassium depletion; bicarbonate worsens the alkalosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Acid-Base Disturbances)

explanation.summary: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is associated with hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from gastric acid and chloride loss. Pre-operative correction with isotonic saline resuscitation followed by 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose and potassium chloride 20 mmol/L is standard. Targets are chloride above 100 mmol/L, bicarbonate below 30 mmol/L, and potassium above 3.5 mmol/L. Surgery is delayed until these targets are met to prevent post-anaesthetic apnoea.
key_points: Pyloric stenosis causes a hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis
Resuscitate with sodium chloride 0.9% then maintenance saline-dextrose plus potassium
Delay surgery until chloride above 100 mmol/L and bicarbonate below 30 mmol/L
explanation.pearls: Failure to correct alkalosis before anaesthesia is the major preventable cause of post-pyloromyotomy apnoea.

subtopic_detail: Suspected inhaled foreign body in a toddler
stem: A 22-month-old presents 6 hours after a sudden coughing fit while eating peanuts during a family barbecue. The choking episode resolved spontaneously but he is now persistently coughing and his mother says his breathing sounds different. He has no preceding upper respiratory tract symptoms, no fevers, no eczema, and no prior wheeze.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 42, T 37.0, SpO2 94% room air
data_table.Examination: Mild intercostal recession, unilateral monophonic wheeze right side, decreased air entry on the right, no stridor at rest, no drooling, no cyanosis
data_table.Investigations: Chest x-ray shows hyperinflation of the right lung with mediastinal shift to the left on expiratory film

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Trial nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes for three doses then reassess for viral-induced wheeze before further imaging
options.rationale: Salbutamol does not relieve mechanical airway obstruction and delays definitive airway clearance, with risk of mucus plugging and dislodgement. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Aspiration)

options.text: Arrange urgent rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia by the on-call paediatric otolaryngology service today
options.rationale: A witnessed choking event with persistent unilateral signs and obstructive emphysema on imaging is diagnostic and warrants same-day rigid bronchoscopy. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Aspiration)

options.text: Perform five back blows and five chest thrusts now in the emergency department then observe in the short-stay unit
options.rationale: Back blows are reserved for the actively choking child unable to cough; this child has air movement and forced manoeuvres risk dislodging the object into the trachea. (Source: ANZCOR: Paediatric Basic Life Support)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days plus inhaled corticosteroid maintenance, with general practitioner review in 1 week
options.rationale: Steroids treat airway inflammation in asthma exacerbations and do not address mechanical obstruction. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

options.text: Discharge with safety-netting after a normal inspiratory chest x-ray report and return if symptoms worsen overnight
options.rationale: Inspiratory films are commonly normal; the expiratory film here already shows mediastinal shift and the clinical scenario is diagnostic. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Aspiration)

explanation.summary: Inhaled foreign body presents with sudden choking, persistent unilateral wheeze, and decreased air entry on the affected side. Expiratory or decubitus chest x-ray shows obstructive hyperinflation with mediastinal shift away from the affected side. Same-day rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia is the diagnostic and therapeutic procedure of choice. Salbutamol and steroids do not relieve mechanical obstruction and may delay definitive airway clearance.
key_points: Choking with persistent unilateral signs is rigid bronchoscopy until proven otherwise
Decubitus or expiratory films reveal obstructive emphysema even when inspiratory films appear normal
Bronchodilators and steroids do not relieve mechanical obstruction
explanation.pearls: Peanuts cause acute lipoid pneumonia if retained; do not delay bronchoscopy waiting for normalisation of breath sounds.

subtopic_detail: Salter-Harris distal tibial fracture in a child
stem: A 12-year-old presents 90 minutes after twisting her right ankle on a trampoline. She heard a pop and could not bear weight to walk to the car. There is no head strike, no other limb injury. She has a previously normal contralateral ankle and no bleeding disorder. The ankle is now swollen with localised tenderness over the medial malleolus.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96, RR 16, T 36.6, BP 110/72, capillary refill less than 2 seconds in the toes, sensation intact
data_table.Examination: Marked swelling around the medial malleolus, point tenderness over the distal tibial physis, unable to bear weight for four steps in the department, neurovascular status intact distally
data_table.Investigations: Ankle radiograph shows a vertical fracture line through the distal tibial epiphysis extending into the physis without metaphyseal involvement; mortise widening 1 mm

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Apply a removable below-knee splint, allow weight-bearing as tolerated, and arrange general practitioner review in 1 week without specialist referral
options.rationale: A Salter-Harris III intra-articular fracture risks growth disturbance and articular incongruity if reduction is inadequate; specialist input is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Ankle Fractures)

options.text: Apply a full below-knee fibreglass cast and discharge with crutches and orthopaedic outpatient appointment within 6 weeks
options.rationale: Outpatient delay over 6 weeks risks malunion and growth arrest; intra-articular incongruity warrants earlier orthopaedic input. (Source: RCH CPG: Ankle Fractures)

options.text: Discharge with a tubular bandage and crutches plus advice to return only if pain or swelling persists beyond 7 days
options.rationale: This is a confirmed intra-articular fracture, not a soft tissue sprain, and tubular bandaging is inadequate immobilisation. (Source: RCH CPG: Ankle Fractures)

options.text: Apply a backslab and arrange same-day orthopaedic review for consideration of computed tomography and reduction of the intra-articular fracture
options.rationale: Salter-Harris III patterns require computed tomography to characterise displacement and possible operative fixation; backslab and same-day orthopaedic review are standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Ankle Fractures)

options.text: Arrange a magnetic resonance imaging scan of the ankle from the emergency department and review the patient in 48 hours
options.rationale: Magnetic resonance imaging is not first-line for an acute Salter-Harris fracture; computed tomography defines bony anatomy more practically. (Source: RCH CPG: Ankle Fractures)

explanation.summary: A vertical epiphyseal fracture extending to the physis without metaphyseal involvement is a Salter-Harris III pattern. These intra-articular fractures need computed tomography to define displacement and orthopaedic review for possible operative fixation. Initial management is a well-padded backslab with neurovascular checks. Long-term follow-up screens for growth arrest at the affected physis.
key_points: Salter-Harris III involves the epiphysis and physis without crossing the metaphysis
Computed tomography defines articular displacement and informs operative planning
Same-day orthopaedic review is required for intra-articular paediatric fractures
explanation.pearls: Growth arrest risk after Salter-Harris III is around 10-20%; counsel families about possible angular deformity at follow-up.

sources.label: Australian Orthopaedic Association: Paediatric Fracture Management

subtopic_detail: Tumour lysis syndrome prophylaxis in high-risk ALL induction
stem: An 8-year-old has been diagnosed with B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukaemia with a presenting white cell count of 88 x10^9/L and bulky cervical adenopathy. The paediatric oncology team plan induction chemotherapy commencing within 24 hours. Pre-chemotherapy bloods show baseline uric acid 0.62 mmol/L, creatinine 96 micromol/L, potassium 4.6, phosphate 1.9 mmol/L, calcium 2.18 mmol/L, and lactate dehydrogenase 1450 U/L.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral 3 cm cervical lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly 4 cm below costal margin, splenomegaly 5 cm, no respiratory compromise, no tumour lysis-related arrhythmia
data_table.Investigations: G6PD assay pending and not yet returned from referral laboratory; weight 26 kg; eGFR estimated 76 mL/min/1.73 m2

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate tumour lysis prophylaxis to commence now?

options.text: Hyperhydrate at twice maintenance with isotonic crystalloid without potassium plus oral allopurinol 100 mg/m2 three times daily, holding rasburicase pending G6PD
options.rationale: Rasburicase causes severe haemolysis in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency; until the assay returns, hyperhydration plus allopurinol is appropriate and rasburicase is reserved if the assay is normal or hyperuricaemia escalates. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL Tumour Lysis)

options.text: Hyperhydrate at twice maintenance with isotonic crystalloid plus alkalinisation with intravenous sodium bicarbonate to maintain urine pH 7.0 to 7.5 throughout induction
options.rationale: Alkalinisation increases calcium phosphate precipitation and is no longer recommended once allopurinol or rasburicase are available. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL Tumour Lysis)

options.text: Commence intravenous rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg once daily for 5 days now and proceed to chemotherapy induction the morning after the first dose
options.rationale: Rasburicase causes severe haemolysis and methaemoglobinaemia in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency; testing should not be bypassed in elective induction. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL Tumour Lysis)

options.text: Restrict fluids to two-thirds maintenance to prevent volume overload and start oral allopurinol 100 mg/m2 once daily with daily renal function and electrolyte review
options.rationale: Fluid restriction worsens tumour lysis nephropathy; hyperhydration with at least 3 L/m2/day is essential. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL Tumour Lysis)

options.text: Initiate intermittent haemodialysis prophylactically from the start of chemotherapy to clear uric acid and prevent acute kidney injury during induction
options.rationale: Renal replacement therapy is reserved for established acute kidney injury, refractory hyperkalaemia, or severe hyperphosphataemia, not as routine prophylaxis. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL Tumour Lysis)

explanation.summary: High-risk paediatric acute lymphoblastic leukaemia carries substantial tumour lysis syndrome risk. Prophylaxis is hyperhydration at twice maintenance with isotonic crystalloid without potassium, plus allopurinol. Rasburicase is reserved for high-risk patients with rising or established hyperuricaemia, but only after glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency has been excluded due to fatal haemolysis risk. Routine alkalinisation is no longer recommended. Renal replacement therapy is reserved for established complications such as refractory hyperkalaemia, severe hyperphosphataemia, or oligo-anuric acute kidney injury.
key_points: Hyperhydration at 3 L/m2/day plus allopurinol is the routine prophylactic platform
Rasburicase is held until glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency is excluded
Avoid alkalinisation; it worsens calcium phosphate precipitation
explanation.pearls: Rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency and pregnancy; sample for assay before any first dose even if the result will take days to return.

stem: A 62-year-old presents with a single episode of spotting 4 days ago, occurring 9 years after her last menstrual period. She has never been on hormone therapy. Her body mass index is 31 and she has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin. There is no family history of endometrial or colorectal cancer. She has had no abnormal cervical screening tests.

data_table.Examination: Speculum reveals a parous atrophic cervix with no obvious cervical lesion; bimanual examination reveals a non-tender mobile uterus of normal size; no adnexal mass
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening 18 months ago: negative for HPV oncogenic strains; pregnancy test not applicable

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test today and review again in 3 months from the general practitioner if no further bleeding occurs in the interim
options.rationale: Cervical screening does not investigate endometrial pathology, which is the priority concern in postmenopausal bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG: Investigation of Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Schedule an outpatient hysteroscopy and dilatation and curettage under general anaesthesia within 6 weeks as the initial investigation
options.rationale: Operative hysteroscopy is escalation after non-diagnostic outpatient imaging or sampling, not the initial step. (Source: RANZCOG: Investigation of Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Arrange transvaginal ultrasound endometrial thickness within 2 weeks with onward referral if thickness exceeds 4 mm or imaging is non-diagnostic
options.rationale: Transvaginal ultrasound measuring endometrial thickness is the first-line investigation; less than 4 mm has a high negative predictive value for endometrial cancer in postmenopausal women. (Source: RANZCOG: Investigation of Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Order serum cancer antigen 125 and a contrast-enhanced computed tomography scan of the abdomen and pelvis for initial workup
options.rationale: CA-125 and computed tomography are not first-line in postmenopausal bleeding; transvaginal ultrasound directs further workup. (Source: RANZCOG: Investigation of Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Reassure as a single bleed without bleeding for 12 months thereafter is a self-limited atrophic event needing no further follow-up
options.rationale: All postmenopausal bleeding warrants investigation; endometrial cancer risk is approximately 10% across the population. (Source: RANZCOG: Investigation of Postmenopausal Bleeding)

explanation.summary: Postmenopausal bleeding requires investigation because the population risk of endometrial cancer is approximately 10%, rising with obesity, diabetes, and unopposed oestrogen exposure. Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line investigation; an endometrial thickness of 4 mm or less in a woman not on tamoxifen has very high negative predictive value. Persistent bleeding or thickness above 4 mm leads to endometrial sampling, usually outpatient pipelle, with hysteroscopy if sampling is inadequate or focal lesions are suspected. Tamoxifen users follow a different pathway because they may have a thickened sub-endometrial complex.
key_points: All postmenopausal bleeding requires investigation regardless of how minor it appears
First-line is transvaginal ultrasound for endometrial thickness
Endometrial thickness of 4 mm or less has high negative predictive value in non-tamoxifen users
explanation.pearls: Obesity, diabetes, and chronic anovulation drive unopposed oestrogen exposure and lift endometrial cancer risk markedly even in non-bleeding women.
